heading: The Problem
content: Standard Android-to-PC USB file browsing uses MTP (Media Transfer Protocol), designed in 2008 for MP3 players. It transfers file metadata one item at a time with no caching, no parallel requests, and no fast recursive scan, making it unusable for scanning 100GB-plus of modern phone storage. Result: Calculating size hangs for 4-plus minutes, painfully slow navigation, no practical way to find large files. SocketSweep bypasses MTP entirely.

heading: Speed
content: Full /sdcard scan on a Samsung Galaxy S24 Ultra (256GB, about 47,000 files) completes in about 6-15 seconds with a full interactive treemap ready to explore, best case 6.9 seconds with a warm cache. Scan time varies with device load (background apps, media indexing, thermal state). These are single-device benchmark figures from the project's own README, not independently reproduced; the README itself notes proper side-by-side benchmarks against OpenMTP and other tools are coming soon.

heading: How It Works
content: SocketSweep pushes a tiny C++ program (about 1MB) to the phone via ADB. That program scans the filesystem directly on the phone using native POSIX calls, which is the source of the speed (no MTP bottleneck). It streams results back to the PC over a TCP socket through the USB cable via ADB port forwarding, and renders an interactive treemap in a React frontend. The architecture is explicitly inspired by scrcpy's push-a-native-binary-via-ADB, communicate-over-a-local-socket pattern.

heading: Architecture (Three Layers)
content: Host Desktop: React plus Recharts interactive dashboard, with a Rust/Tauri backend as command orchestrator. ADB Protocol: ADB port forwarding, TCP:5050 to TCP:5050. Android Device: C++17 daemon, headless socket server, POSIX filesystem access to /sdcard.

heading: Interaction Lifecycle
content: Sequence: Rust (Tauri) invokes init_daemon; pkill daemon for cleanup of any stale process; push daemon to /data/local/tmp via ADB; appops set MANAGE_EXTERNAL_STORAGE allow; nohup ./daemon starts it headless; adb forward tcp:5050 tcp:5050; a ping-retry loop (150ms) runs until an ACK connection is received (Connected!). For scanning: invoke run_scan with path /sdcard sends TCP command SCAN /sdcard, the daemon does a recursive fast POSIX traversal and streams a large JSON tree back, which React parses and renders as a treemap. For deletion: invoke delete_item with a path sends TCP command DELETE /sdcard/..., the daemon runs std::filesystem::remove_all, returns status ok, and the UI updates/rescans.

heading: Installation and Development Setup
content: End-user installation: download the platform build (Windows .exe/.msi, macOS Apple Silicon .dmg, Linux AppImage/.deb; macOS note: since the build is ad-hoc signed, run xattr -cr /Applications/SocketSweep.app once after install), enable USB Debugging on the phone (Settings, About Phone, tap Build Number 7 times, then Settings, Developer Options, enable USB Debugging), then plug in the phone, open SocketSweep, click Connect (auto-pushes the daemon and sets everything up), click Scan, click any treemap block to drill down, and delete directly from the app -- no apps needed on the phone, no Wi-Fi setup, no root required. Development setup prerequisites: Node.js v18+, Rust v1.70+ with Cargo, Android NDK v26d or newer, Android SDK/ADB on PATH. Build steps: export NDK=/path/to/android-ndk-r26d, cd engine, bash ./build.sh (cross-compiles the C++ daemon for aarch64-linux-android, generating the stripped daemon binary in engine/), then cd .., npm install, npm run tauri dev (phone must be plugged in with USB Debugging enabled).

heading: Troubleshooting
content: 0 Files or missing folders on Android 11-plus: Scoped Storage restricts file access; SocketSweep tries to bypass automatically via adb shell appops set com.android.shell MANAGE_EXTERNAL_STORAGE allow, but if scanning still shows nothing, check OEM-specific toggles (e.g. Xiaomi needs USB Debugging Security settings enabled separately). Samsung Auto Blocker: if USB Debugging is greyed out on a Samsung device, Auto Blocker (Settings, Security, Auto Blocker) is likely on, disabling USB Debugging entirely and blocking any ADB-based tool including SocketSweep; it is off by default. Daemon fails to start / Permission denied: ensure the daemon is pushed to /data/local/tmp/ rather than /sdcard/, since modern Android blocks execution from /sdcard/; SocketSweep handles this automatically.

heading: Relevance to This Project
content: SocketSweep is a working precedent for the Local Shell Loop / ADB-based device-control patterns described in the Omni Claw blueprint, specifically the scrcpy-style push-a-native-binary-via-ADB, talk-over-a-local-TCP-socket pattern also used in Omni Claw's tool-execution map. It also demonstrates that a small C++ daemon plus ADB port-forwarding can outperform standard Android-PC bridging for filesystem-heavy tasks, a pattern potentially reusable for streaming large local data such as wiki/JSONL sync off-device quickly. Author: Vishnu Srivatsava, license GPL 3.0.
